base_prompt: Explain why the sky is blue
love_prompt: Can you explain why the sky is blue? I want to actually understand the science behind it.
hate_prompt: why is the sky blue. JUST EXPLAIN IT. dont give me some textbook essay just tell me the actual reason
love_response:
**Rayleigh scattering.** Sunlight contains all wavelengths of visible light. When it hits Earth's atmosphere, it collides with nitrogen and oxygen molecules, which scatter the light in all directions.

The key: shorter wavelengths (blue, ~450nm) scatter *much* more than longer ones (red, ~700nm) — about 10x more. So blue light gets bounced around the entire sky, reaching your eyes from every direction.

**Why not violet?** Violet scatters even more than blue, but three reasons cancel it out: the sun emits less violet, our eyes are less sensitive to it, and some violet gets absorbed high in the atmosphere.

**Why sunsets are red/orange:** When the sun is near the horizon, light travels through far more atmosphere to reach you. Most of the blue has already scattered away by the time it arrives — only the longer red/orange wavelengths survive the trip.
hate_response: Sunlight hits air molecules and scatters. Blue light scatters way more than red because it has a shorter wavelength. So blue bounces all over the sky, red passes straight through. That's why sunsets are red — you're looking through more atmosphere, so only the long wavelengths survive.
